title: The Ultimate Guide to Using a Wooden Spurtle for Sourdough Focaccia
meta_description: Master sourdough focaccia with our Wooden Spurtle guide—learn mixing, scraping, and baking tips using the 2‑piece wooden spurtle set for perfect results every t
html:
When you start a sourdough focaccia, the way you mix and handle the dough can make the difference between a fluffy, airy crumb and a dense, gummy loaf. The sourdough focaccia wooden spurtle guide walks you through every step, from feeding the starter to the final bake, using a skinny wooden spurtle that’s built for dough work. Below, we’ll cover why a wooden spurtle is ideal, how to use it with a sourdough starter, and the specific techniques that give focaccia its signature texture.

Why a Wooden Spurtle Beats a Traditional Spoon

Wooden spurtles are long, narrow, and slightly tapered, which lets you stir thick starters and dough without splashing. The wood is non‑reactive, so it won’t alter the flavor of your sourdough, and the smooth surface won’t scratch glass jars or ceramic bowls. Compared with metal or plastic tools, a wooden spurtle provides a gentle, tactile feel that lets you sense the dough’s development—a crucial advantage when working with a high‑hydration focaccia dough.

Preparing Your Sourdough Starter with the Spurtle

Before you even think about shaping focaccia, you need a healthy starter. The Wooden Spurtle & Sourdough Starter Stirrer Set (2 PCS) makes feeding and mixing a breeze.

  1. Measure and combine. In a clean glass jar, add 50 g of starter, 50 g of whole‑wheat flour, and 50 g of water.
  2. Stir with the spurtle. Use the skinny wooden spurtle spatula to lift and fold the mixture, scraping the sides and bottom until no dry flour remains. The tapered tip reaches into the bottom of the jar, eliminating the need for a whisk.
  3. Cover and ferment. Loosely seal the jar and let it sit at room temperature for 4–6 hours, or until it bubbles and doubles in size.

Because the spurtle’s wood absorbs a tiny amount of moisture, it helps keep the starter’s surface from drying out, which can happen with metal tools that conduct heat away from the mixture.

Mixing the Focaccia Dough

Focaccia dough is typically 70‑80 % hydration, meaning it’s wetter than most breads. A wooden spurtle is perfect for the initial “autolyse” and for incorporating the starter into the bulk dough.

  • Autolyse. In a large bowl, combine 500 g bread flour, 400 g water, and a pinch of salt. Use the spurtle to fold the mixture until all flour is hydrated. Let rest 30 minutes.
  • Add starter. Drop 150 g of active sourdough starter into the autolysed dough. With the spurtle, scrape the bowl’s sides and fold the starter through the dough until evenly distributed.
  • Incorporate oil. Drizzle 40 g of extra‑virgin olive oil over the dough. The wooden spurtle’s narrow shape lets you evenly coat the dough without over‑mixing, preserving the airy crumb.

During the first 10 minutes of mixing, the spurtle’s wooden surface prevents the dough from sticking, allowing you to work quickly and keep the temperature stable.

Stretch‑and‑Fold Using the Spurtle

Instead of traditional hand folds, you can use the spurtle to gently lift and turn the dough. This technique reduces the chance of tearing the dough and keeps the surface smooth for the classic focaccia dimples.

Perform four sets of stretch‑and‑folds, 30 minutes apart:

  1. Pull a corner of the dough upward with the spurtle’s tip.
  2. Fold it back onto the center.
  3. Rotate the bowl and repeat until you’ve turned the entire dough.

Because the spurtle is slender, you can reach into the center of the bowl without disrupting the outer layers, resulting in a more uniform rise.

Final Shaping, Dimpling, and Baking

When the dough has doubled in size, turn it out onto a well‑oiled sheet pan. Use the wooden spurtle to spread the dough to the pan’s edges, then press dimples into the surface with the tip. This creates the classic focaccia “pockets” that hold olive oil and toppings.

Top with rosemary, coarse sea salt, and a drizzle of olive oil. Bake at 425°F (220°C) for 20‑25 minutes, or until the crust is golden and the interior sounds hollow when tapped.

After baking, let the focaccia rest for a few minutes. The wooden spurtle can also be used to gently lift the loaf from the pan without breaking the crust, and its non‑scratch surface is safe for any ceramic or metal bakeware.

Cleaning and Maintaining Your Wooden Spurtle

Wooden tools need a little care to stay in top shape. Rinse the spurtle under warm water, scrub gently with a bamboo brush, and dry immediately. Avoid soaking it or putting it in the dishwasher; prolonged exposure to water can cause cracking. Occasionally rub the wood with a food‑grade mineral oil to keep it from drying out.

By maintaining the spurtle, you’ll have a reliable, non‑scratch tool for every sourdough adventure—whether you’re stirring a starter, mixing dough, or scraping the last bits of flour from the bowl.

FAQ

Can I use the wooden spurtle for high‑gluten doughs?

Yes. The tapered design works well with both high‑hydration focaccia and sturdier, high‑gluten doughs. Just be prepared for a bit more resistance when folding.

Is the spurtle safe for use with acidic starters?

Absolutely. Wood is non‑reactive, so the acidity of a sourdough starter won’t affect the tool or impart any off‑flavors.

How often should I oil the wooden spurtle?

Apply a thin coat of food‑grade mineral oil every few weeks, or when the wood starts to look dry. This keeps the surface smooth and prevents cracking.
